document: Joint Standard 2 of 2024  Cybersecurity & Cyber resilience Requirements

sections.heading: Definitions and interpretation
sections.title: Definitions of cybersecurity terms and financial institution scope
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used throughout the Joint Standard, including 'cybersecurity', 'cyber incident', 'financial institution', and 'cyber resilience'. It specifies that 'financial institution' includes banks, insurers, pension funds, and other entities regulated under South African financial laws. The definitions clarify the scope of cybersecurity requirements for these institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides foundational definitions critical for understanding cross-border data transfers, as 'financial institution' scope includes entities handling international data flows. Clarifies what constitutes 'cyber incident' and 'cyber resilience' relevant to data protection.

requirements.text: ‘the Act' means the Financial Sector Regulation Act, 2017
‘financial institution' includes banks, insurers, pension funds, and other entities regulated under South African financial laws

sections.relevance_reason: Defines key terms for financial institutions under data compliance

sections.heading: Application
sections.title: Application scope for financial institutions
sections.summary: This section specifies that the Joint Standard applies to financial institutions as defined in the standard. It requires banks and their controlling companies to mitigate cybersecurity risks from all juristic persons and branches, including foreign entities. Insurers and their controlling companies must similarly address cybersecurity risks from their structured entities. The standard must be tailored to the institution's size, complexity, and risk profile.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines which financial institutions must comply with cybersecurity requirements, including cross-border entities. Critical for determining data transfer obligations when financial institutions operate internationally.

requirements.text: Banks and controlling companies must mitigate cybersecurity risks from all juristic persons and branches, including foreign entities
Insurers and controlling companies must mitigate cybersecurity risks from all juristic persons and branches, including foreign entities
Minimum requirements must reflect the nature, size, complexity and risk profile of the financial institution

sections.relevance_reason: Financial data cybersecurity risk mitigation for cross-border entities

sections.heading: Roles and responsibilities
sections.title: Roles and responsibilities for cybersecurity governance
sections.summary: This section outlines that the governing body is ultimately responsible for ensuring compliance with the Joint Standard and overseeing cyber risk management. It requires the governing body to establish a robust cybersecurity strategy with senior management, facilitate cooperation with stakeholders, and ensure clear roles for security in third-party contracts. The governing body must also oversee the implementation of cybersecurity measures.
sections.dtia_summary: Clarifies governance responsibilities for cybersecurity, critical for data transfer impact assessments involving third parties and international operations.

requirements.text: Governing body must ensure compliance with the Joint Standard
Governing body must oversee cyber risk management and delegate primary oversight to a committee
Governing body must establish a sound cybersecurity strategy with senior management
Governing body must ensure cooperation with stakeholders for financial sector cyber resilience
Governing body must ensure clear roles for security in third-party contracts

sections.relevance_reason: Cybersecurity governance for financial data compliance

sections.heading: Governance
sections.title: Cybersecurity governance structures
sections.summary: This section requires financial institutions to define roles and responsibilities for management functions and committees overseeing cyber risks. It mandates that cyber risk management be integrated into the institution's governance framework, with direct reporting lines to the governing body. It also requires a dedicated function for cybersecurity with adequate resources and authority, and ensures that oversight functions have access to the governing body without conflicts of interest.
sections.dtia_summary: Establishes governance structures critical for data transfer impact assessments, particularly for international data flows and third-party data sharing.

requirements.text: Define roles and responsibilities of all management functions and committees for cyber risk oversight
Integrate cyber risk management into governance and risk management structures
Establish a function responsible for cybersecurity with adequate resources and authority
Ensure oversight functions have direct access to the governing body
Ensure segregation of duties and avoid conflicts of interest in oversight functions

sections.relevance_reason: Cybersecurity governance for financial data compliance

sections.heading: Cybersecurity strategy and framework
sections.title: Cybersecurity strategy and framework requirements
sections.summary: This section mandates that financial institutions establish and maintain a cybersecurity strategy approved by the governing body, aligned with business strategy. It requires regular review of the strategy, establishment of a cybersecurity framework, alignment with enterprise risk management, development of cybersecurity policies, and regular reassessment of business risk tolerance. Metrics must be established to track cybersecurity risks and inform reporting.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides detailed requirements for cybersecurity strategies, critical for assessing data transfer risks when financial institutions operate internationally.

requirements.text: Establish and maintain a cybersecurity strategy approved by the governing body
Review cybersecurity strategy at least annually to address threats, allocate resources, and remediate gaps
Establish a cybersecurity framework to manage cyber risks
Align cybersecurity framework with enterprise risk management framework
Establish cybersecurity policies, standards, and procedures informed by industry best practices
Define and reassess business risk tolerance relative to cybersecurity
Establish metrics to track and manage cybersecurity risks

sections.relevance_reason: Cybersecurity strategy for financial data compliance

sections.heading: Cybersecurity and cyber resilience fundamentals
sections.title: Cybersecurity fundamentals for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must identify critical business processes and information assets, classify them by criticality and sensitivity, conduct security risk assessments, and maintain updated inventories of information assets. They must implement robust cybersecurity practices to prevent, limit and contain cyber incidents, including identity and access management, data security, application and system security, network security, cryptography, and cybersecurity awareness training. These measures must be regularly reviewed and updated to address evolving threats.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes foundational cybersecurity requirements that directly impact data transfer and processing across borders by mandating robust security controls, encryption, and incident response protocols. Financial institutions must ensure data security and integrity during cross-border transfers through these fundamental security practices.

requirements.text: Identify business processes and information assets supporting business and service delivery
Classify business processes and information assets by criticality and sensitivity
Conduct security risk assessments on critical operations and information assets
Maintain inventory of information assets including location, ownership, and roles/responsibilities
Update inventory when changes are required and review at least biennially
Implement cyber resilience capabilities to prevent, limit and contain cyber incidents
Limit access to information assets to authorized users, processes, and devices
Manage access based on assessed risk of unauthorized access
Establish identity management and access control mechanisms for user administration, accountability and authentication
Establish security and access control policies and procedures
Ensure remote access is only from secured devices meeting security standards
Implement strong authentication for remote access users
Develop data loss prevention policies for sensitive information in motion, at rest or in use
Implement measures to prevent and detect unauthorized access, modification, copying, transmission or theft of data
Ensure third-party service providers' information assets receive same protection level as critical assets
Encrypt or protect sensitive information with access controls commensurate to risk exposure
Use only authorized IT systems, endpoint devices and storage for sensitive information
Implement controls to prevent unauthorized internet services for sensitive data
Restrict use of sensitive information in non-production environments unless equivalent controls are in place
Implement controls to manage access and removal of sensitive information to prevent leaks
Mask sensitive information in production and non-production environments
Permanently delete sensitive information from storage media before disposal or redeployment
Have agreement for secure return or transfer of data upon contract termination
Implement non-disclosure or confidentiality provisions in agreements with users
Implement security-by-design approach to minimize system vulnerabilities
Determine acceptable security level for business needs and assess threats and risks
Specify security requirements for access control, authentication, transaction authorization, data integrity, system logging, audit trails and exception handling
Review and test changes to business-critical applications to ensure no adverse impact
Install network security devices to secure connections with internet and third-party providers
Deploy network intrusion detection or prevention systems
Review network architecture and interconnections periodically to identify vulnerabilities
Implement network access controls to detect and prevent unauthorized devices
Review network access mechanisms at least annually
Review firewall rules periodically and test network perimeter controls annually
Isolate internet web browsing activities from sensitive IT systems
Encrypt remote connections to prevent data leakage through sniffing
Establish cryptographic key management policies, standards and procedures
Adopt cryptographic algorithms from well-established international standards
Ensure cryptographic keys are securely generated and protected from unauthorized disclosure
Use secure key destruction method for expired or revoked keys
Determine appropriate cryptographic key lifespan based on sensitivity and risks
Securely replace cryptographic keys before expiration
Maintain backups of cryptographic keys with high protection
Ensure cryptographic algorithms have been rigorously tested or vetted
Establish comprehensive cybersecurity awareness training program
Conduct user refresher training at least annually
Ensure governing body receives cybersecurity training
Review cybersecurity training program periodically for relevance
Maintain effective cyber resilience capabilities to monitor and detect cyber events
Periodically evaluate effectiveness of security controls through monitoring, testing and audits
Establish security monitoring capabilities including security operations center
Define processes, roles and responsibilities for security operations
Establish process to collect, review and retain IT system logs
Protect IT system logs against unauthorized access, editing and deletion
Configure IT system events or alerts for early indication of security issues
Actively monitor security events or alerts to address issues early
Correlate multiple events on IT system logs to identify suspicious activity patterns
Establish process for timely escalation of suspicious activities
Implement capabilities to rapidly respond and recover from cyber-attacks
Establish cyber incident management policies and processes
Develop data backup strategy with regular backups and testing
Ensure sensitive information in backup media is secured (e.g. encrypted)
Store backup media offline or at offsite location (including cloud storage)
Implement communication strategy for customers impacted by cyber-attacks
Set RPO and RTO based on nature, scale, complexity and risk profile
Establish cyber incident response and management plan
Describe communication, coordination and response procedures for cyber threats
Establish process to investigate security control deficiencies causing compromise
Evaluate full extent of impact from security incidents
Use cyber intelligence and lessons from incidents to enhance security controls
Test cyber incident response and management plan against plausible threats
Understand threat landscape and implications in operating environment
Establish process to collect and analyze cyber-related information for business relevance
Implement cyber intelligence monitoring capabilities for internal and external threats
Participate in cyber threat information-sharing arrangements with trusted external parties
Test all elements of cyber resilience capacity and security controls
Determine testing frequency based on vulnerability rates and criticality
Ensure security control assurance is provided by appropriately skilled personnel
Escalate and report to governing body security control deficiencies that cannot be remediated
Ensure remediation plan with timelines is followed for identified control deficiencies
Conduct regular vulnerability assessments on IT systems and information assets
Address vulnerabilities in a timely manner
Determine vulnerability assessment frequency based on criticality and exposure
Conduct penetration testing on critical IT systems and information assets
Determine penetration testing frequency based on criticality and exposure
Validate security controls for internet-accessible systems after major changes
Conduct penetration testing at least annually if no major changes
Carry out regular scenario-based simulation exercises to validate response and recovery
Design simulation exercises using threat intelligence relevant to IT environment
Test security functionality on web-based and critical applications during development
Adopt standards on secure coding, source code review and application security testing
Establish policy and procedure for third-party and open-source software code review
Review policy and procedures regularly
Establish comprehensive remediation process to track and resolve issues
Classify issues by severity and prioritize based on risk
Set timeframes for remediation of issues of different severity
Conduct risk assessments where appropriate
Implement mitigation strategies for deviations from cybersecurity framework
Track all issues identified from cybersecurity testing or exercises
Remediate known major issues and security flaws before production deployment
Track updates and reported vulnerabilities on in-house, third-party and open-source software

sections.relevance_reason: Core cybersecurity and data protection requirements for financial institutions

sections.heading: Cybersecurity and cyber resilience fundamentals (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: Cybersecurity fundamentals directly impact data protection

sections.heading: Cybersecurity hygiene practices
sections.title: Cybersecurity hygiene practices for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must implement robust access management policies including strong password controls, regular policy reviews, user access provisioning processes, segregation of duties, least privilege principles, periodic access reviews, and consistent access controls for third parties. These requirements ensure appropriate user access rights and minimize unauthorized access risks to critical systems and information assets.
sections.dtia_summary: These practices establish baseline security controls critical for DTIAs involving financial data transfers, particularly regarding access management and third-party vendor controls.

requirements.text: Establish a security access control policy and process for strong password security controls
Review security access control policy regularly
Establish user access management process for provisioning, changing and revoking access rights
Apply segregation of duties and least privilege principles when granting user access
Ensure appropriate parties perform periodic user access reviews to verify privileges
Apply same monitoring and access restrictions to third-party service providers as employees
Secure administrative accounts to prevent unauthorized access
Grant privileged account access on a need-to-use basis with logging and review
Establish process to manage and monitor privileged accounts for suspicious activity
Implement MFA for users with access to critical system functions
Implement MFA or privilege access management for all administrative accounts
Implement MFA for all user accounts accessing internet applications with sensitive information
Protect network from unauthorized access and disruption
Implement security controls at network perimeter to restrict unauthorized traffic
Adopt defense in depth approach with multiple layers of security controls
Apply security patches within timeframe commensurate with vulnerability risks
Institute compensating security controls where patches unavailable
Test security patches before production application to ensure compatibility
Implement remediation plan with timelines for incompatible patches
Establish written security standards for hardware and software
Ensure security standards minimize exposure to cyber threats
Review security standards periodically for relevance and effectiveness
Verify uniform application of security standards and identify deviations
Institute controls to reduce risk from non-conformity to security standards
Implement endpoint protection against malware delivery channels
Keep anti-malware signatures up-to-date and regularly scan systems
Implement detection and response mechanisms for indicators of compromise

sections.relevance_reason: Core cybersecurity and financial data access controls

sections.heading: Notifications and regulatory reporting
sections.title: Material incident reporting requirements for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must notify the responsible authority within specified timeframe after classifying cyber incidents or information security compromises as material incidents. Additional reporting obligations require institutions to submit information related to compliance with the Joint Standard as determined by the authorities, with reporting formats and timelines specified by regulatory notices.
sections.dtia_summary: These reporting requirements impact DTIAs by establishing incident classification thresholds and mandatory notification procedures for security breaches involving financial data.

requirements.text: Notify responsible authority after classifying cyber incidents as material incidents
Notify responsible authority after classifying information security compromises as material incidents
Report information related to Joint Standard compliance to authorities as determined

sections.source_quote: A financial institution must notify the responsible authority in the form and manner determined by the Authorities, after classifying the following as a material incident – cyber incidents; or information security compromise.
sections.relevance_reason: Material incident reporting for financial data breaches

sections.heading: Short title and commencement
sections.title: Effective date and title of the Joint Standard
sections.summary: This section establishes the official title of the Joint Standard as 'Cybersecurity and Cyber Resilience Requirements for Financial Institutions, 2024' and specifies that it comes into effect on a date determined by the authorities through a regulatory notice published on their websites.
sections.source_quote: This Joint Standard is called Cybersecurity and Cyber Resilience Requirements for Financial Institutions, 2024 and comes into effect on the date indicated in paragraph 10.2 below.
